item.title: A View on FOSDEM 2020
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Another year, another FOSDEM edition. As always, since this conference grew so big (fact: if you tried to watch all videos in a row, it'll take you about 9 weeks!), chances are, every review you read from the conference will contain something different, and therefore, complementary.

This is what I was able to exôÙpeôÙriôÙence. Let's take a look.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 3 minutos de lectura)

item.title: Notes on the Kafka Summit London 2018
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
These are some notes and takeôÙaways on the reôÙcentôÙly celôÙeôÙbratôÙed KafôÙka SumôÙmit 2018 in LonôÙdon.

The conôÙferôÙence was orôÙgaôÙnized in three parôÙalôÙlel tracks for sesôÙsions that were covôÙerôÙing stream proôÙcessôÙing, pipeliôÙnes, and inôÙterôÙnalôÙs. To get a good exôÙpeôÙriôÙence, I atôÙtendôÙed talks of the three typeôÙs, but with a litôÙtle prefôÙerôÙence toôÙwards inôÙterôÙnals and streamôÙs.

It was a two-ôÙday conôÙferôÙence with lots of valuôÙable techôÙniôÙcal conôÙtent, aweôÙsome talkôÙs, speakôÙerôÙs, and a lot more. Here are the highôÙlightôÙs.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 1 minutos de lectura)

item.title: PyCon CZ - Day 2
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
SumôÙmaôÙry of the secôÙond day of PyôÙcon CZ 2017.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 2 minutos de lectura)

item.title: PyCon CZ - Day 1
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
The day started at 9.00, and at first it was time of check-in, etc. After that, I solved one riddle by kiwi.com, and earned a discount in flights, which was a nice way to start the conference.

Then afôÙter breakôÙfast and some netôÙworkôÙing goôÙing through the booths of the sponôÙsors, it was time for the first talk of the day: "When bugs bite - why neôÙglectôÙing edge casôÙes can kill".

It was a great talk, a case for softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing in genôÙerôÙal (it's not Python-speciôÙfic, which is what makes the topôÙic more inôÙterôÙestôÙing). The best thing I liked about the talk was the reôÙmarkôÙs, and the idea of shiftôÙing the mindôÙset when it comes to deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt, so we should do some "negôÙaôÙtive thinkôÙing" in the form of what can go wrong with this? How can this fail?, and such. It's imôÙporôÙtanôÙt, beôÙcause this is critôÙiôÙcal in orôÙder to make roôÙbust softôÙware. Most probôÙlems I have seen on softôÙware bugs were reôÙlatôÙed to some opôÙtiôÙmistic thinkôÙing, and even worse, opôÙtiôÙmistic unit testôÙing, in the way that deôÙvelôÙopôÙers onôÙly test for hapôÙpy-ôÙpath sceôÙnarôÙios, withôÙout thinkôÙing all sort of things that can go wrong.

AfôÙterôÙwardôÙs, was the time for the talk about parôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing (poor perôÙsonôÙ's parôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing), which was fine. Then I spent some time tackôÙling some of the chalôÙlenges sponôÙsors had availôÙable, so I did some codôÙing and reôÙcap of the events so far.

Then I listened to a talk about wolfcrypt which is a tool for crypto in Python. The talk introduced some crypto concepts (symmetric crypto, public key, etc.), which was good. Most of the questions revolved around comparison with other tools in python (default libraries, pycrypto, etc.).

Then it was time for lunch and do some more codôÙing, and the next talk I atôÙtendôÙed to was called "should I mock or should I not?", which I liked very much and gave some food for thought.

Then it came one more talk, afôÙter which it was time for mine, so I preôÙsentôÙed clean code in Python. There were some inôÙterôÙestôÙing quesôÙtionôÙs, and the enôÙtire preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion went relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly quickôÙly.

Once the talk sesôÙsions were over, there was one last track for lightôÙning talkôÙs, which are alôÙways suôÙper enôÙterôÙtainôÙing.

AfôÙter the finôÙish of the first day, I atôÙtendôÙed the speakôÙer's dinôÙner, which was a nice opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to netôÙwork with the comôÙmuôÙniôÙty, which is alôÙways great.

LookôÙing forôÙward to some more inôÙterôÙestôÙing talks toôÙmorôÙrow, and to see the reôÙsults of the chalôÙlenges.

item.title: My talk @ EuroPython 2016
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
I had the great exôÙpeôÙriôÙence of preôÙsentôÙing at EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016. My talk enôÙtiôÙtled "Clean code in Python", was about good deôÙvelôÙopôÙment pracôÙtices, down to low-levôÙel deôÙsign (with code exôÙamôÙples), for Python. The idea of the talk, was to present the "pythonôÙic" apôÙproach for writôÙing code, and how do genôÙerôÙal conôÙcepts of clean code apôÙply to Python.

These exôÙamôÙples might be useôÙful for beôÙginôÙnerôÙs, deôÙvelôÙopôÙers exôÙpeôÙriôÙenced in othôÙer lanôÙguages comôÙing to Python, and peoôÙple usôÙing Python for sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic apôÙpliôÙcaôÙtionôÙs. The exôÙamôÙples could alôÙso be helpôÙful for seôÙnior deôÙvelôÙopôÙerôÙs, beôÙcause they reôÙmind reôÙal sitôÙuôÙaôÙtions that might apôÙpear in pull reôÙquestôÙs, while doôÙing a code reôÙview.

Here is the video on YouTube:

And the slides (which I alôÙso made availôÙable along with the source code, shortôÙly afôÙter the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion finôÙished).

The preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion was well reôÙceived: some atôÙtenôÙdees told me they liked it (even asked for the slides and code), and I got good adôÙvices. The folôÙlowôÙing days of the conôÙferôÙence, more peoôÙple told me that they liked the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion, and some othôÙers menôÙtioned (someôÙthing I did not think at the beôÙginôÙning, but that it makes perôÙfect sense), that these ideas are reôÙalôÙly useôÙful for peoôÙple usôÙing Python in sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic enôÙviôÙronôÙments.

I am glad it was useôÙful for the comôÙmuôÙniôÙty.
